fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the i18n routing utility for the static export project. The core idea is that we have a translation catalog (a nested dictionary keyed by language code) and we need a small runtime library that handles all the typical locale stuff — picking the right language, building navigation links, rendering translated strings, that kind of thing. We talked about this back when we did the browser-detection piece, so carry that pattern forward here.

The tricky parts I keep hearing about from the frontend folks: when someone's browser reports a regional variant like 'en-US', we should gracefully handle that (I think we strip the region part? check how we did it before). Also there are some edge cases around what happens when the URL has a language code we don't recognize — we want a sensible fallback rather than a broken page.

There's also the language switcher component — it needs to be accessible (screen readers etc.) and correctly wire up the router navigation, including preserving any other query params already in the URL. Oh and template strings in translations should support some kind of variable interpolation.

The outputs need to match exactly what the test harness expects — I know there are some subtle formatting differences between the 'resolved via browser' path vs the 'normal config' path so please double check those. Reach out if anything is unclear.

One quick follow-up since a few details came back from the team. On the browser-default path, when use_browser_default=true and browser_language has a regional bit like 'foo-US', we should split on '-' and only use the primary subtag 'foo'. If that primary subtag is actually present as a key in the translations dictionary, that becomes the resolved default_language. If it is not in the catalog, we keep the configured default_language and use the non-resolved-via-browser output instead of the resolved one.

Also, the output shape really does change depending on whether that browser resolution actually worked. If use_browser_default is true and the browser language after stripping the region matches a catalog key, use the resolved format exactly as 'config_default_language=<lang>\navailable_languages=<sorted>\ntranslation.<lang>.<key>=<val>\n...' — note 'config_default_language' (not 'default_language') and all translation key-value pairs are listed in sorted order. If browser detection is turned on but the browser language does not match any catalog key, then use the unresolved format exactly as 'default_language=<lang>\nbrowser_language=<raw>\nbrowser_default_enabled=true\navailable_languages=<sorted>\n'. And when use_browser_default is false, use that same unresolved format with browser_default_enabled=false.

There are also three separate config error cases and they should be checked in order, since they are mutually exclusive. If the translations dictionary is empty, the output is exactly 'error=missing_translations\n' and nothing else. If default_language is an empty string, the output is exactly 'error=missing_default_language\n'. If default_language is non-empty but is not a key in the translations dictionary, the output is 'error=invalid_default_language\ndefault_language=<value>\n'.

For selected language resolution, please include a source field alongside selected_language. If router_query has a lang key and that value exists in the translations dictionary, output 'selected_language=<lang>\nsource=query\n'. If router_query is empty, null, or has a lang value that is not in the translations dictionary, output 'selected_language=<defaultLang>\nsource=default\n'. And for router_query_sequence, treat the first entry as initial_selected_language, the second as updated_selected_language, and if the second query had a lang that got ignored because it was not in the catalog, report that as ignored_query_lang.

On query composition, the precedence is strict: forced_language if present and non-empty wins and becomes the lang value first. If that is not set, use selected_language if non-empty. If selected_language is empty, then keep the existing router_query lang value. All other keys from router_query stay unchanged. If router_query is null and selected_language is empty and there is no forced_language, return {}. The output key here is query_json, and the value should be compact JSON with keys in insertion order.

Last thing, just repeating the core bit for feature1_configuration_resolution because this was where the confusion was: when use_browser_default=true, split browser_language on '-', take the primary subtag, check whether it exists in the translations dictionary keys, and only then override the resolved default_language. If it does not exist, keep the configured default and stick with the unresolved output format.

trigger_keywords: active state logic for language switcher
is_active determination from route vs default
when router_query is null or empty for active check
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Active state is determined by two branches: if router_query is null OR router_query does not contain a 'lang' key (including empty object {}), then is_active = (target_language == default_language). If router_query contains a 'lang' key, then is_active = (target_language == router_query.lang). The router_lang output field is the raw value of router_query.lang or empty string if absent/null. Both null and {} router queries produce router_lang= (empty).

trigger_keywords: template interpolation syntax for translations
how to substitute variables in translation strings
mustache double curly brace syntax
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Template strings in the translation catalog use double-curly-brace syntax: {{variableName}}. When a lookup_key resolves to a string containing {{...}} placeholders and template_data is provided, each placeholder is replaced with the corresponding value from template_data. The translation_json output wraps the result in JSON (strings get quoted, arrays and objects are serialized as compact JSON, numbers in template data are substituted as their string representation).

trigger_keywords: nested key lookup with dot notation
resolving deeply nested translation values
fallback when key path does not exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Translation keys support dot-separated path navigation (e.g. 'levelOne.levelOneString' traverses {levelOne: {levelOneString: 'value'}}). If any segment of the path does not exist in the translation object for the selected language, the output returns the original requested key string as the fallback value (wrapped in JSON quotes). Arrays and plain objects that are terminal values are returned as their compact JSON serialization.

trigger_keywords: language switcher accessibility attributes
aria label format for language switch button
role attribute on language switch control
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The language switch control must render with role=button and aria_label='set language to <targetLanguage>'. The aria_label format is exactly 'set language to ' followed by the target language key string. When a child element is nested inside the control and that child has its own click handler, the child's click handler must still be called when the control is activated (child_click_handler_called=true). The router push must always include the composed query with 'lang' set to the target language, the current pathname (undefined if not set), and the shallow flag as provided.

trigger_keywords: available languages sort order in output
how are language keys ordered in catalog output
alphabetical sorting of language codes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The available_languages field and the translation.* enumeration in the resolved-config output are both sorted alphabetically by language code. For example, if the catalog has keys 'mock' and 'foo', the output lists them as 'foo,mock' (alphabetical ascending). Translation key-value pairs within each language block are also enumerated in alphabetical order by the dot-path of the key.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: subtle formatting differences between the resolved-via-browser path vs the normal config path
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Two distinct output templates exist: the 'resolved' template uses the key 'config_default_language' and enumerates all translation key-value pairs; the 'unresolved' template uses 'default_language', 'browser_language', 'browser_default_enabled', and 'available_languages' without enumerating individual translation values. See feature1_configuration_resolution.json cases 0 vs 1-3.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The router_query null-safety pattern: throughout all features (selected language, active state, query composition), null router_query and empty-object router_query {} must be treated equivalently as 'no language in route', falling back to the configured default_language rather than throwing an error.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — please check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output contract, or look at how similar edge cases are handled in the feature files already in the repo.

feature1_configuration_resolution.json.description: Resolves and validates a supplied language catalog before any localized output is requested.

feature2_selected_language.json.description: Selects the active language from route data while falling back to the configured default when the route is absent or unsupported.

feature3_language_query_composition.json.description: Builds a navigation query object that preserves existing query fields and chooses the language value using the documented precedence.

feature4_language_switcher_active_state..description: Reports whether a language switch target is active from the route language when present, otherwise from the configured default language.

feature5_translation_lookup.json.description: Looks up localized values by key path, renders template strings with provided data, and returns the requested key when no value exists.

feature6_language_switch_control.json.description: Renders an accessible language switch control and, when activated, sends a route update containing the target language and requested shallow-routing flag.
